Its management is to treat the cause and to restrict water intake, with more active measures — careful sodium correction, and vasopressin-receptor antagonists — for severe cases, always correcting the sodium gradually to avoid osmotic demyelination. So the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone is excess antidiuretic-hormone effect causing euvolaemic dilutional hyponatraemia with concentrated urine, with many perioperative causes, managed by treating the cause and restricting water, correcting sodium gradually — an endocrine cause of the hyponatraemia discussed with fluids.

qa.q: Describe the aldosterone antagonists.
qa.a: The aldosterone antagonists, or mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonists, oppose the action of aldosterone at its receptor in the renal collecting duct, reducing sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion, so they act as potassium-sparing diuretics and antihypertensives, and have important roles beyond simple diuresis. Spironolactone is the prototype; eplerenone is more selective with fewer endocrine side effects. They are used in primary hyperaldosteronism; in resistant hypertension; and, importantly, in heart failure, where they reduce mortality, and in the ascites of liver disease. Their characteristic adverse effect is hyperkalaemia, from the potassium-sparing action, particularly with renal impairment or other potassium-raising drugs, requiring monitoring; spironolactone also causes anti-androgenic effects such as gynaecomastia, which eplerenone largely avoids. So the aldosterone antagonists spironolactone and eplerenone block the mineralocorticoid receptor, acting as potassium-sparing diuretics and antihypertensives with a key mortality benefit in heart failure, their main risk being hyperkalaemia and, for spironolactone, anti-androgenic effects — the pharmacological counterpart to mineralocorticoid physiology.

qa.q: How long does the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis take to recover after steroids?
qa.a: Recovery of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis after prolonged corticosteroid therapy is slow and variable, which is why steroids are tapered and why cover is given for a period after stopping. The suppression depends on the dose and duration: short courses of a week or two cause little lasting suppression, but prolonged supraphysiological therapy suppresses the axis such that, after the steroid is withdrawn, the pituitary and adrenal may take many months — sometimes up to a year — to recover their full responsiveness, with the pituitary recovering before the adrenal. During this recovery period the patient remains at risk of being unable to mount an adequate cortisol response to stress, so they may need steroid cover for surgery even some months after stopping long-term steroids, and abrupt cessation is avoided. So the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis recovers slowly after prolonged steroids — over months and sometimes up to a year — so patients remain at risk of an inadequate stress response and may need perioperative cover for months after stopping, underlining the need for gradual withdrawal and a careful steroid history.

qa.q: Thank you. A thorough and well-structured account. Let us stop there. Before we close, here are the take-home messages. First, Cortisol is the adrenal glucocorticoid under hypothalamic-pituitary control, secreted in a circadian and stress-responsive way, with metabolic (gluconeogenesis, hyperglycaemia), anti-inflammatory, immunosuppressive, cardiovascular-permissive and mineralocorticoid effects. 2. Synthetic steroids differ in glucocorticoid potency, mineralocorticoid activity and duration: hydrocortisone (replacement/cover), prednisolone (oral anti-inflammatory), dexamethasone/betamethasone (potent, no mineralocorticoid — antiemesis, cerebral/airway oedema), fludrocortisone (mineralocorticoid replacement); they act via an intracellular receptor altering gene transcription, hence a delayed onset. Second, Uses include replacement, anti-inflammatory/immunosuppression, antiemesis, airway and cerebral oedema, anaphylaxis adjunct, perioperative cover and antenatal fetal lung maturation; adverse effects of prolonged use are an iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome (hyperglycaemia, osteoporosis, immunosuppression, peptic ulcer, mood change) plus mineralocorticoid fluid retention and hypokalaemia. Third, Prolonged steroids suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, so the patient cannot mount a stress cortisol response — give perioperative hydrocortisone cover scaled to surgery to prevent an Addisonian crisis (refractory hypotension, hypoglycaemia, electrolyte disturbance), treated urgently with IV hydrocortisone, fluids and electrolyte/glucose correction. Fourth, Thyroid disease: levothyroxine for hypothyroidism (continue perioperatively); carbimazole/propylthiouracil with beta-blockers for hyperthyroidism (render euthyroid before surgery to avoid thyroid storm; agranulocytosis is the serious risk); etomidate inhibits 11-beta-hydroxylase, causing transient adrenal suppression — used cautiously in the critically ill. Thank you. That concludes the station.
